title: Download 60,000 Works of Art from the National Gallery, Including Masterpieces by Van Gogh, Gauguin, Rembrandt & More
description: As a young amaôÙteur painter and future art school dropout, I freôÙquentôÙly found myself hauntôÙed by the faces of two artists, that famousôÙly odd c
body:
As a young amaôÙteur painter and future art school dropout, I freôÙquentôÙly found myself hauntôÙed by the faces of two artists, that famousôÙly odd couôÙple from my favorite art hisôÙtoôÙry novelizationãand Kirk DouôÙglas role and Iggy Pop songãLust for Life. VinôÙcent van Gogh and Paul GauôÙguin, above and below respecôÙtiveôÙly, the torôÙmentôÙed Dutch fanatôÙic and burly French bullyãhow, I still wonôÙder, could such a pair have ever co-existôÙed, howôÙevôÙer briefly? How could such beauôÙtiôÙfulôÙly skewed visions of life have existôÙed at all?
Van Gogh and Gauguinãs sevôÙerôÙal self-porôÙtraits still inspire wonôÙder. My younger self had the luxôÙuôÙry of seeôÙing these parôÙticôÙuôÙlar two up close and in perôÙson at the NationôÙal Gallery of Art in WashôÙingôÙton, DC: Van Goghãs gaunt and piercôÙing visôÙage, Gauguinãs sneerôÙing self-parôÙoôÙdy.
Now, thanks to the wonôÙders of digôÙiôÙtal techôÙnolôÙoôÙgy, my oldôÙer self, and yours, can view and downôÙload high-resôÙoôÙluôÙtion phoôÙtos of both paintôÙings, and over 60,000 more from the museumãs vast holdôÙings, through NGA Images, ãa reposôÙiôÙtoôÙry of digôÙiôÙtal images of the colôÙlecôÙtions of the NationôÙal Gallery of Art.ã

There youãll find works by anothôÙer obsesôÙsive Dutch self-porôÙtraitist, RemôÙbrandt van Rijn, such as the lush 1659 paintôÙing below. Youãll find paintôÙings from the heroes of the varôÙiôÙous RenaisôÙsances and French ImpresôÙsionôÙism, from moveôÙments modôÙern and coloôÙnial, pasôÙtoral and urban. The colôÙlecôÙtion is dizzyôÙing, and a lover of art could easôÙiôÙly lose hours sortôÙing through it, savôÙing ãopen access digôÙiôÙtal images up to 3000 pixôÙels each [ãÎ] availôÙable free of charge for downôÙload and use.ã The purôÙpose of NGA Images is ãto facilôÙiôÙtate learnôÙing, enrichôÙment, enjoyôÙment, and exploôÙration,ã and thereãs no doubt that it satôÙisôÙfies all of those goals and then some.

Browse the varôÙiôÙous colôÙlecôÙtions, includôÙing one devotôÙed to self-porôÙtraits. ConôÙduct advanced searchôÙes, if you have more knowlôÙedge of the Galleryãs many treaôÙsures. You are the curaôÙtor! And the lucky benôÙeôÙfiôÙciaôÙry of the NationôÙal Galleryãs benefôÙiôÙcence. While I can tell you from expeôÙriôÙence that itãs nothôÙing like standôÙing face to face with these paintôÙings in their in-real-life dimenôÙsions, texôÙtures, lines, and colorsãdespite the throngs of disôÙinôÙterôÙestôÙed touristsãitãs at least a close secôÙond. And for stuôÙdents and eduôÙcaôÙtors of the visuôÙal arts, NGA Images offers an opporôÙtuôÙniôÙty like no othôÙer to view and share great works of art often hidôÙden away from even the museumãs visôÙiôÙtors. Enter the colôÙlecôÙtion here.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2014.
If you would like to sign up for Open Cultureãs free email newsletôÙter,ô please find it here. Itãs a great way to see our new posts, all bunôÙdled in one email, each day.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:

Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: Bertrand Russellãs Advice For How (Not) to Grow Old: ãMake Your Interests Gradually Wider and More Impersonalã
description:
in Life, Philosophy |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Image by NationôÙal PorôÙtrait Gallery, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
Advice on how to grow
body:
in Life, Philosophy |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Image by NationôÙal PorôÙtrait Gallery, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
Advice on how to grow old freôÙquentôÙly comes from such banal or bloodôÙless sources that we can be forôÙgivôÙen for ignorôÙing it. PubôÙlic health offiôÙcials who disôÙpense wisôÙdom may have good intenôÙtions; pharôÙmaôÙceuôÙtiôÙcal comôÙpaôÙnies who do the same may not. In either case, the mesôÙsages arrive in a form that can bring on the despair they seek to avert. ElderôÙly peoôÙple in well-lit phoôÙtographs stroll down garôÙden paths, ballôÙroom dance, do yoga. BulôÙletôÙed lists puncôÙtuôÙatôÙed by dry citaôÙtions issue genôÙtly-wordôÙed guideôÙlines for senôÙsiôÙble livôÙing. InofôÙfenôÙsive blandôÙness as a preôÙscripôÙtion for livôÙing well.
At the othôÙer extreme are proôÙfiles of excepôÙtionôÙal casesãrelatively spry indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals who have passed the cenôÙtuôÙry mark. Rarely do their stoôÙries conôÙform to the modôÙel of abstemiousôÙness enjoined upon us by proôÙfesôÙsionôÙals. But we know that growôÙing old with digôÙniôÙty entails so much more than diet and exerôÙcise or makôÙing it to a hunôÙdred-and-two. It entails facôÙing death as squareôÙly as we face life. We need writôÙers with depth, senôÙsiôÙtivôÙiôÙty, and eloôÙquence to delivôÙer this mesôÙsage. Bertrand RusôÙsell does just that in his essay ãHow to Grow Old,ã writôÙten when the philosoôÙpher was 81 (sixôÙteen years before he evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly passed away, at age 97).
RusôÙsell does not flatôÙter his readôÙersã ratioôÙnalôÙist conôÙceits by citôÙing the latôÙest sciôÙence. ãAs regards health,ã he writes, ãI have nothôÙing useôÙful to sayãÎ. I eat and drink whatôÙevôÙer I like, and sleep when I canôÙnot keep awake.ã (We are inclined, perôÙhaps, to trust him on these grounds alone.) He opens with a driôÙly humorôÙous paraôÙgraph in which he recôÙomôÙmends, ãchoose your ancesôÙtors well,ã then he issues advice on the order of not dwelling on the past or becomôÙing a burôÙden to your chilôÙdren.
But the true kerôÙnel of his short essay, ãthe propôÙer recipe for remainôÙing young,ã he says, came to him from the examôÙple of a materôÙnal grandôÙmothôÙer, who was so absorbed in her life, ãI do not believe she ever had time to notice she was growôÙing old.ãô ãIf you have wide and keen interôÙests and activôÙiôÙties in which you can still be effecôÙtive,ã RusôÙsell writes. ãyou will have no reaôÙson to think about the mereôÙly staôÙtisôÙtiôÙcal fact of the numôÙber of years you have already lived, still less of the probôÙaôÙble shortôÙness of your future.ã
Such interôÙests, he argues, should be ãimperôÙsonôÙal,ã and it is this qualôÙiôÙty that loosens our grip. As Maria PopoôÙva puts it, ãRusôÙsell places at the heart of a fulôÙfillôÙing life the disôÙsoôÙluôÙtion of the perôÙsonôÙal ego into someôÙthing largôÙer.ã The idea is familôÙiar; in Russellãs hands it becomes a medôÙiôÙtaôÙtion on morôÙtalôÙiôÙty as ever-timeôÙly as the so-often-quotôÙed pasôÙsages from Donneãs ãMedôÙiôÙtaôÙtion XVII.ãô PhilosoôÙpher and writer John G. MesserôÙly calls Russellãs conôÙcludôÙing pasôÙsage ãone of the most beauôÙtiôÙful reflecôÙtions on death I have found in all of world litôÙerôÙaôÙture.ã
The best way to overôÙcome it [the fear of death]ãso at least it seems to meãis to make your interôÙests gradôÙuôÙalôÙly wider and more imperôÙsonôÙal, until bit by bit the walls of the ego recede, and your life becomes increasôÙingôÙly merged in the uniôÙverôÙsal life. An indiôÙvidôÙual human exisôÙtence should be like a rivôÙer: small at first, narôÙrowôÙly conôÙtained withôÙin its banks, and rushôÙing pasôÙsionôÙateôÙly past rocks and over waterôÙfalls. GradôÙuôÙalôÙly the rivôÙer grows wider, the banks recede, the waters flow more quiôÙetôÙly, and in the end, withôÙout any visôÙiôÙble break, they become merged in the sea, and painôÙlessôÙly lose their indiôÙvidôÙual being. The man who, in old age, can see his life in this way, will not sufôÙfer from the fear of death, since the things he cares for will conôÙtinôÙue. And if, with the decay of vitalôÙiôÙty, weariôÙness increasôÙes, the thought of rest will not be unwelôÙcome. I should wish to die while still at work, knowôÙing that othôÙers will carôÙry on what I can no longer do and conôÙtent in the thought that what was posôÙsiôÙble has been done.
Read Russellãs ãHow to Grow Oldã in full here. And see many more eloôÙquent medôÙiôÙtaôÙtions on aging and deathãfrom HenôÙry Miller, Andrûˋ Gide, UrsuôÙla K. Le Guin, and Grace Paleyãat The MarôÙginôÙaôÙlian.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent: ô 
Bertrand Russellãs Advice to PeoôÙple LivôÙing 1,000 Years in the Future: ãLove is Wise, Hatred is FoolôÙishã
Bertrand RusôÙsell: The EveryôÙday BenôÙeôÙfit of PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy Is That It Helps You Live with UncerôÙtainôÙty
Simone de Beauvoirãs PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy on FindôÙing MeanôÙing in Old Age
Youãre Only As Old As You Feel: HarôÙvard PsyôÙcholôÙoôÙgist Ellen Langer Shows How MenôÙtal AttiôÙtude Can PotenôÙtialôÙly Reverse the Effects of Aging
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: Seven Wonders of the Ancient World: From the Walls of Babylon to the Sewers of Rome
description:
in History |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

You may not be able to name all, or even most, of the sevôÙen wonôÙders of the ancient worl
body:
in History |   	March 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

You may not be able to name all, or even most, of the sevôÙen wonôÙders of the ancient world. But you almost cerôÙtainôÙly know that there were sevôÙen of them. In a way, that aligns well enough with the worldôÙview of the Greeks who first made refôÙerôÙence to such a list, givôÙen their near-revôÙerôÙence for that numôÙber. SevôÙen were the strings of the lyre (unless there hapôÙpened to be eight or nine), sevôÙen were the gates of Thebes, and sevôÙen were the ãwanôÙderôÙing starsã in the night sky (if you count the sun and moon). The idenôÙtiôÙty of the wonôÙders was less imporôÙtant than the length of their list, and indeed, as ancient-hisôÙtoôÙry YouTuôÙber GarôÙrett Ryan explains in his Told in Stone video above, addiôÙtions and changes were proôÙposed since the beginôÙning.
The clasôÙsic sevôÙen-wonôÙders rosôÙter includes the HangôÙing GarôÙdens of BabyôÙlon, the StatôÙue of Zeus at Olympia, the TemôÙple of Artemis at EphôÙesus, the MauôÙsoleum at HaliôÙcarôÙnasôÙsus, the ColosôÙsus of Rhodes, the LightôÙhouse of AlexanôÙdria, and the Great PyraôÙmid of Giza, that last being the only one still in exisôÙtence today.
Ryanãs alterôÙnaôÙtive list includes the EgyptôÙian labyrinth at Hawara, which Herodotus conôÙsidôÙered supeôÙriôÙor even to the PyraôÙmids; the TemôÙple of Zeus at CyzôÙiôÙcus, which Pliny the Elder described as lined by gold tubes to let in the sunôÙlight (sureôÙly stripped out as soon as the place fell into disôÙuse); the sewôÙers of Rome, a civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtionôÙal achieveôÙment unto themôÙselves; and the TheôÙater of ScauôÙrus, which, though conôÙstructôÙed out of wood for temôÙpoôÙrary use, seatôÙed an astonôÙishôÙing 80,000 peoôÙple.
Ryan comôÙpletes his sevôÙen othôÙer wonôÙders with the Altar of Horns at Delos, held in myth to have been built by ApolôÙlo himôÙself; the Walls of BabyôÙlon, which actuôÙalôÙly appear on the earôÙliôÙest known verôÙsion of the list; and, finalôÙly, the good old ColosôÙseôÙum. As over-familôÙiar (not to menôÙtion over-toured) as it may be, the FlaôÙvian AmphitheôÙater, as the ColosôÙseôÙum was known in its day, does make for a welôÙcome presôÙence among the ancient wonôÙders, being the only othôÙer one apart from the Great PyraôÙmid that we can still visôÙit today. But if you get into the mood to go marôÙvel at thauôÙmaôÙta, to borôÙrow the Greek word, by no means limôÙit yourôÙself to selecôÙtions already curatôÙed by othôÙers. The world is full of wonôÙders, and your own perôÙsonôÙal sevôÙen may not be far away.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:

Ten Lost Roman WonôÙders: The Worldãs Longest TunôÙnel, Tallest Dam, Widest-SpanôÙning Bridge & More

Take a VirôÙtuôÙal Tour of Machu PicôÙchu, One of the New 7 WonôÙders of the World
The Ancient World Comes to Life in an AniôÙmaôÙtion FeaôÙturôÙing Istanbulãs IslamôÙic, Ottoman, Greek & ByzanôÙtine Art
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The First Robot Movie: Watch a Newly Discovered Georges Mûˋliû´s Film from 1897
description:
in Film, History |   	March 4th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

MetropôÙoôÙlis,ô ForôÙbidôÙden PlanôÙet, 2001: A Space Odyssey, Star Wars, Blade RunôÙner,ô The T
body:
in Film, History |   	March 4th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

MetropôÙoôÙlis,ô ForôÙbidôÙden PlanôÙet, 2001: A Space Odyssey, Star Wars, Blade RunôÙner,ô The TerôÙmiôÙnaôÙtor,ô Short CirôÙcuit, RoboôÙCop, Ghost in the Shell,ô The Iron Giant, WALLãE, Ex MachiôÙna: there is a parôÙalôÙlel hisôÙtoôÙry of cinôÙeôÙma to be told entireôÙly through its robots.ô That such a hisôÙtoôÙry must begin with the work of Georges Mûˋliû´s may not come as a surôÙprise, givôÙen that he inventôÙed so many of the techôÙniques of sciôÙence-ficôÙtion filmôÙmakôÙing. But until recentôÙly, we didôÙnãt actuôÙalôÙly know that the cinôÙeôÙma pioôÙneer who ãinventôÙed everyôÙthingã ever put a robot onscreen. The eviôÙdence turned up among a colôÙlecôÙtion of ãold and batôÙteredã reels of film that were ãfrom before World War I and had been shutôÙtled around from baseôÙments to barns to garages and had just been dropped off at the Library.ã
So writes the Library of ConôÙgressã Neely TuckôÙer, who goes on to describe the action of one of the films involvôÙing ãa magiôÙcian and a robot batôÙtling it out in slapôÙstick fashôÙion. It took a bit, but then the gasp of realôÙizaôÙtion: They were lookôÙing at ãGugusse and the AutomaôÙton,ã a long-lost film by the iconôÙic French filmôÙmakôÙer Georges Mûˋliû´s at his Star Film comôÙpaôÙny.ã
Mûˋliû´s himôÙself plays the magiôÙcian, who ãwinds up an automaôÙton dressed like the famous clown PierôÙrot, which is standôÙing on a pedestal. Once wound up, the clown begins to beat the magiôÙcian with his walkôÙing stick. The magiôÙcian retalôÙiôÙates by getôÙting a huge sledgeôÙhamôÙmer and bashôÙing the automaôÙton over the head, with each blow seemôÙing to shrink it in half, until it is just a small doll.ã
In just 45 secôÙonds, this simôÙple film would have astonôÙished audiôÙences back in 1897 ã and indeed retains the powôÙer to impress, proôÙvidôÙed you conôÙsidôÙer that none of the techôÙniques to realôÙize its effects were wideôÙly known before Mûˋliû´s attemptôÙed them. He did so five years before ãA Trip to the Moon,ã a hugeôÙly ambiôÙtious cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic endeavôÙor by comôÙparôÙiôÙson, and by far the sinôÙgle film that best repôÙreôÙsents his legaôÙcy.ã Yet it and Gugusse and the AutomaôÙton are clearôÙly the work of the same artist-invenôÙtor, one who posôÙsessed that rare comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion of techôÙniôÙcal know-how and artisôÙtic darôÙing, and who underôÙstood the need for an organôÙic relaôÙtionôÙship between specôÙtaôÙcle and narôÙraôÙtive. Not that either the specôÙtaôÙcle or the narôÙraôÙtive are highôÙly evolved at this stage, but, as Mûˋliû´s may have susôÙpectôÙed, the cinôÙeôÙma of robots has as long an evoôÙluôÙtion ahead of it as automaôÙta themôÙselves.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Watch 194 Films by Georges Mûˋliû´s, the FilmôÙmakôÙer Who ãInventôÙed EveryôÙthingã (All in ChronoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal Order)
How Georges Mûˋliû´s A Trip to the Moon Became the First Sci-Fi Film & Changed CinôÙeôÙma ForôÙevôÙer (1902)
The Word ãRobotã OrigôÙiôÙnatôÙed in a Czech Play in 1921: DisôÙcovôÙer Karel áapekãs Sci-Fi Play R.U.R. (a.k.a. Rossumãs UniôÙverôÙsal Robots)
Fritz Lang First DepictôÙed ArtiôÙfiôÙcial IntelôÙliôÙgence on Film in MetropôÙoôÙlis (1927), and It FrightôÙened PeoôÙple Even Then
Watch ãThe Birth of the Robot,ã Len Lyeãs SurôÙreôÙal 1935 Stop-Motion AniôÙmaôÙtion
Watch the Sci-Fi Short Film ãIãm Not a Robotã: WinôÙner of a 2025 AcadôÙeôÙmy Award
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The Met Introduces High-Definition 3D Scans of Dozens of Art Historical Objects
description: In the age of the internet, weãre fortunate to have virtual access to museum collections around the world, thanks to objects in the public domain
body:
In the age of the internet, weãre fortunate to have virtual access to museum collections around the world, thanks to objects in the public domain and programs like The Metropolitan Museum of Artãs Open Access Initiative. Through a searchable digital catalogue, visitors to the museumãs website can see hundreds of thousands of objects, many images of which are available for download. And itãs not aloneãother institutions like the Art Institute of Chicago, The National Gallery of Art, and The Cleveland Museum of Art, among others, make pieces in their collections accessible to all.
The thing is, digital images donãt always give us the full picture, so to speak. Even two-dimensional paintings and drawings have unique textures, structural details, and materials that we can only really appreciate in person. This wonãt ever really changeãnothing beats the real thing. But one caveat is that even in person, much of the work remains hidden. We canãt see the backs of oil paintings, for example, and edges are often hidden within frames. Thanks to The Metãs continued emphasis on imaging, we can now experience every detail in three-dimensional renderings of nearly 140 significant objects in its holdings.
The Temple of Dendur (10th century B.C.E.)
The Met is home to a whopping 1.5 million historic objects, which range from the iconic Temple of Dendur and Impressionist paintings to African tribal ceremonial sculptures and medieval pottery. The museum recently published 3D models of some of these, plus numerous other objects, including nine produced in collaboration with NHK (Japan Broadcasting Corporation).
With careful attention to technical precision and color, these animated renderings are research-grade tools, allowing us to see the objects at any angle. View van Goghãs brushstrokes closer than youãre allowed to in a museum, zoom in on a Babylonian cuneiform tablet, and turn an 18th-century Turkish tile over to see its reverse side.
The Met plans to continue adding 3D scans to its online library. Explore more on the museumãs website.

schema.headline: The Met Introduces High-Definition 3D Scans of Dozens of Art Historical Objects

title: Johan Siggesson's Striking Portraits of 'Big Tuskers' in Kenya
description: Among African elephants, ãBig Tuskersã refers to bulls that grow tusks so long they sometimes scrape the ground. Each one can weigh well over 100
body:
Among African elephants, ãBig Tuskersã refers to bulls that grow tusks so long they sometimes scrape the ground. Each one can weigh well over 100 pounds. These giant, ivory incisors continually grow throughout an elephantãs life, and males typically have much larger tusks than females.
The bigger the tusks, however, the more vulnerable these gentle giants are to poachers who harvest and traffic the ivory for trade. There are only a couple dozen left in nature preserves like Kenyaãs Tsavo East National Park and Amboseli National Park.
ãTogether Foreverã
For wilderness photographer Johan Siggesson, a fascination with animals and their habitats led to a series of striking black-and-white photographs of elephants and their herds in Kenya. From charming juveniles to big tuskers like Craig (1972-2026) and emerging contenders like the 35-year-old Michael, Siggesson captures the incredible anatomy, habitat, and behavior of these endangered megafauna.
Prints are available for purchase on Siggessonãs website. Explore more of his work on Instagram.
ãFace to Faceã
ãMichaelã
ãHot Pursuitã
ãCraig & Treeã
ãAt the Foot of the Mountainã

title: Rediscover a Rembrandt After More than Six Decades in Hiding
description: In 1898, Amsterdamãs Stedelijk Museum staged an exhibition of paintings by renowned Dutch Golden Age artist Rembrandt (1606-1669). Included in thi
body:
In 1898, Amsterdamãs Stedelijk Museum staged an exhibition of paintings by renowned Dutch Golden Age artist Rembrandt (1606-1669). Included in this show was a 23-by-19-inch oil painting titled ãVision of Zacharias in the Temple,ã which was completed in 1633, relatively early in the artistãs career. Fast-forward to 1960, and the work was deemed to have not actually been made by Rembrandt. Despite that in the past it had been catalogued as part of his oeuvre, that was no longer the case. So, a private collector purchased it in 1961, from which point on, it remained out of sightãuntil now.
Experts and conservators at the Rijksmuseum, which was recently granted the opportunity to reassess the painting by its owner, spent two years investigating the pigments, structural materials, Rembrandtãs signature and date, the overall style, and the choice of subject matter. Techniques included X-raying the paint and conducting dendrochronology tests on the wooden panels to get a sense of age.

Macro scans also revealed insights into the artistãs creative and technical choices as he worked. ãAn important piece of evidence that the painting is by Rembrandt lies in the changes he made during the painting process,ã the museum says. ãFor example, the censer [an incense burner] on the altar was originally larger. Such adjustments are characteristic of an artist who is experimenting and refining his work. A copyist, by contrast, would have reproduced the model exactly, without making alterations.ã
The scene depicts the high priest Zacharias at the moment just before the archangel Gabriel arrives to tell him that he and his wife will have a son, John the Baptist. Though dim in photographs, a light in the upper right corner of the canvas indicates Gabrielãs imminent arrival. In this charged moment, Rembrandt uses a narrative device known in Dutch as ãstaetveranderinge,ã which translates to ãchanges of state.ã Itãs an element Rembrandt often incorporated in his paintings around this time.
The Rijksmuseum also recently spent five years thoroughly researching and restoring ãThe Night Watch.ã If youãre in Amsterdam, ãVision of Zacharias in the Templeã goes on view today at the Rijksmuseum, where it will remain on long-term loan. Find more on the museumãs website.


A reproduction of ãVision of Zacharias in the Templeã from a 1908 catalogue raisonnûˋ

schema.headline: Rediscover a Rembrandt After More than Six Decades in Hiding
